[04:00] Okay? If you do something too frequently, it fatigues and you dry it out. Everybody familiar? You remember grade school with how clouds and water works? When you have water that gets off the ground, it evaporates into the clouds. The clouds feed the water sources on the ground like rivers and aquafers and streams and ponds and lakes and shit like that.
[04:30] And then when it again gets sucked into the clouds, it just repeats that cycle and continues to refill the water sources and they never really run dry. Then people like us come along and we siphon that water source to a major city of some kind. What happens to that water source as time goes on and those cycles continue? Somebody say it.
[04:50] It disappeared. Say it louder. It disappeared. Congratulations. You're our first winner of a standard iPhone 16. We appreciate your participation.

[68:50] Meaning on a ticket sale to the challenge, we spent just shy of 300 grand. We were 1.9x profitable on the ticket sales. Okay. On the back end, we did you got paid less commission on it, right? For the challenge, just for the direct to cardart.
[69:20] Cool. So, what happened on the challenge is is they sold a 10k offer, but they cash collected a 2k deposit on the call. They then handed those calls off to the salespeople who got paid half of their normal commission. Okay? Okay. So, it also is a little bit higher of a gross event because of that cut in commission on gross dollars.
[69:50] Okay. 2K out of 10K was cash collected on the spot as a deposit. Those people were then told to book. The people who still didn't buy were also told to book. They cleaned the house. They did 3.4 mil off the 10K sales on that specific event. Okay. Since then, we've had people do smaller variations of that who didn't want to take a $300,000 ad spend bet. How'd it play out for you?

[86:45] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Call Funnel Best Practices"
NÚMEROS: 30-day vs 60-day look-back windows, 3:1 ROAS → 5:1 ROAS over time
VISUAL: Comparação de janelas de tempo, gráfico de ROI crescente ao longo do tempo, representação de sales cycle
[86:50] Okay, so next thing, apparently he's an X. Let's go through callunnel best practices. Um, call funnels are still absolutely ripping. We love call funnels. I just want to be clear when I say this. You got to have a great sales team to get call funnels to work. Okay, this has always been the contingency when you get people that are early on in the sales process.
[87:20] A lot of you judge your call funnels based on the first calls that happen and the close rates for them and you never actually expand the time frame to look at how much revenue goes into them in a broader time frame beyond just that single call. Okay, as an example of this, you always want to be thinking with the credit card billing cycle, of course.
[87:50] Okay, when I front cash, I want to make that cash back by the time I owe AMX some money. See what I'm saying? I absolutely want to judge my ROI and make it as big as I can in that time frame. But what we've seen with call funnels nowadays especially is we're getting a lot more conversions through them and we see a much higher ROI as just another like week or two goes beyond the four-week cycle.
[88:20] If we have a look back window, meaning like let's use the example of callunnel within 30 days is at a three to one rorowaz. If we look back at it 60 days later, it might have gone up to like a five to one rorowaz. Does that make sense? Not everybody buys in such a short duration of time when they come through a call funnel because it's early in the sales process when you're usually communicating to somebody through a call funnel.
[88:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Look-Back Window Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Standard: 7-14 days, Extended: 30-60 days shows true performance
VISUAL: Timeline de conversão estendida, gráfico de conversões ao longo do tempo, representação de follow-up effectiveness
[88:55] Same thing with a lead form strategy like the Hydra. Followup, warming them up, long form content consumption. All of these things dramatically increase the willingness and the quantity of people that are going to buy. But most of you don't do look back windows beyond a 30-day time frame. Does that make sense?
[89:25] Yeah. What's the best way to like accurately judge the rorowaz as you're perpetually scaling? Because you're essentially like again judge it within the credit card billing cycle. So as an example, if you have a normal sales process that you can reflect on, let's say it's seven days or 14 days. That's the time frame that you generally want to judge it by first
[89:55] And then you want to just extend it out from there to see how much does it go up at all. Right? Right? Because if it doesn't go up at all, that means you have no follow-up occurring. And that means you have a terrible, terrible follow-up strategy. You need to build out email sequences, retargeting, add additional conversion mechanisms, train your sales team how to actually follow up, things like that. But always judge it by the standard buying cycle that you already that you already have right now.
[90:25] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Cost of Growth Concept"
NÚMEROS: Example client: $1.4M/month, Target: 40% profit margin, Reality: 20-30% while scaling
VISUAL: Gráfico de profit margin vs crescimento, representação de trade-off, comparação de cenários
[90:30] But if you continue to dump more money into your life, artificially look lower as you're scaling. Now, that's a very that's a very great frame right there. So this is actually a very common misconception when you're scaling. Okay, companies don't factor in the the cost of growth.
[91:00] Okay, so if I just spent the same amount of money every month, I could be much more critical of my rorowaz because there's no cost of growth factored into my current rorowaz. Yeah. Okay. The main game here is to be able to be ROI positive and have it eat a little bit into your grow into that rorowaz for your growth being supplemented by house money. Wish Daniel was here. I'd roast him, right? He's missing. You make sure to tell him I said that when he comes back. The cost of growth. Okay. That's what you factor into that same rorowaz formula we just talked about. Does that make sense?
[91:40] Yeah. Usually, I'll give you a great example. We had a client recently that wanted to be at what what was the uh percentage you wanted to be at? Oh, 40%. They wanted to be at a 40% profit margin. They're at 1.4 million a month. Okay, great. Stop growing. Just spend the same amount of money every month. No doubt you can optimize your way to 40%.
[92:10] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Growth vs Optimization Trade-off"

VISUAL: Comparação de cenários, gráfico de trade-off, representação de escolha estratégica
[92:15] But what happens when you want to grow the business at the same time? You're going to sacrifice a little bit of that profit margin. Not much. Usually in the what? High 20s, low 30s, low 20s in the worst month. What's that? For your profit margin.
[92:45] Oh, yeah. Yeah. Yeah. 25 even 20. So long story short, somewhere in the 20s is where they usually fall at. They want to get to 40. Can you do both at the same time? Sometimes to be fair. Yeah. Sometimes. I've been pressing them to add an additional funnels as an example to help supplement that. I think it's possible a few other funnels that could be higher ROI
[93:15] Like the webinar. That's a great example. Challenge funnels, uh, low ticket to high ticket, those could all be examples of that. But here's my point, right? Cost of growth. That's a perfect example of it. If the business at 1.4 four mil a month decides, well, I got to stop and optimize for profit margin. I got to get it up to 40. Great. Well, you either have to do different funnels that are higher profit or you just have to stop growing.
[93:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "True ROAS Calculation"
NÚMEROS: Total spend - scaled dollars = true ROAS, Scaled dollars = cost of growth
VISUAL: Equação de cálculo, separação de investimento base vs crescimento, fórmula visual
[93:55] You have to stop sacrificing the additional dollars to growth and instead be more conservative and you'll essentially just stay stuck when you do that. Does that make sense? Yeah. How do you like judge is this the cost of growth or is the campaign just dimension? Well, usually again, if you're scaling, if you just take out the scaled dollars, you usually get it a reflection of true rorowaz in that example. Does that make sense? So, like over a 30-day duration. Exactly. Just subtract what you scaled into and that's your true rorowaz. That's as easy as it is.
[94:30] The other dollars that you spent that eat into that rorowaz is the cost of growth in that example. Okay. Uh, shout out to Josh on this one. Josh Troy, just to be clear in terms of credit on this, um, I can't remember where he said this or what the frame was, but it was really good. He had said, uh, "Oh man, I have a really long buying cycle. Like, what do I do?"
[95:00] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "10-Day Buying Frame"
NÚMEROS: 10 days = commitment window, In or out by day 10
VISUAL: Timeline de 10 dias, representação de commitment, estrutura de follow-up intensivo
[95:05] And Josh was like, "Oh, tell them about the 10-day buying frame." And I've seen this work so tremendously well after this tip. I had to share it with y'all. Very simple. Okay. If I have a longer buying cycle than 10 days or I want my buying cycle to be a certain amount of days, I'm going to tell people when they come through our buying process, I'm going to say, "Listen, Edward, we have a goal here. It's very simple. I want you to be in or out by the end of 10 days.
[95:40] I want to get you any and all information I can possibly get you to help you make a buying decision by the end of this 10 days. I just want to be clear before we continue. Does that sound fair and agreeable? Very fair. Great. Okay. To commit to that process, I'm going to send you emails that have follow-up information. I'm going to get you any and I'm your point of contact. Get you any and all information that I can to help you make the decision. I don't care whether you're in or out at the end of 10 days. I obviously want to help you and sell you, but I want you to have enough info to not drag it on. Does that make sense?

[102:50] Really? So even if what information is missing? Why would he drag it on? If he gave us a real reason if he was like, "Look, man. I'm doing a fucking real estate deal. I can't have a dollar leave my account because I'm doing a refi right now. I got until Tuesday of next week on this date." We'd be like, "Great. You gave us a real reason, man. We'll respect that.
[103:20] Back two years later. I promise we'll tell him to get fucked I promise we'll tell him to get fucked What if you went through like the spiritual journey? I genuinely don't give a fuck You could have taken Iawasa and claimed you changed your whole life. I don't give a fuck You if you look again, we vet based on traits and characteristics. If you are that way, we will not sell to you. Okay?
[103:50] That's all that it is. Again, be honest with us. Get the information you need or just tell us, hey, it's not for me. That's fine. But we want you in or out, okay? [134:55] Okay. I want to be clear on this too. Uh some of you, as an example, you might send like four emails a day or six emails a day. As ridiculous as this might sound, increase the frequency until the open rate starts to drop. Then you find out what the true frequency cap is. There's no other way to find it out. You have to kind of push it to the limit and then pull it back a little bit if you go over the limit. Does that make sense?
[135:30] Feedback from some random person that says, "You sent me too many emails." That's not what I said. I said, "When the frequency exceeds the point where your open rates diminish, that's when we know we hit the actual limit. That's when we know how much communication on average people want to see from us." That make sense? Yes. Yes. Questions on that?

[139:00] Okay, we have this document which is in our master SOP list for all of you on how to overcome ad fatigue. Some of you have your own interpretations of this document for how you then go about executing it or you ignore it in its entirety. This document is from on a month-by-month basis millions of dollars in spend and overcoming ad fatigue and keeping costs as efficient as possible. And some of you still ignore it and neglect it. Some of you have been in here for years where I've shared this exact protocol and still ignore it and neglect it.
[139:40] This is not me forcing my way on to you. This is me sharing with you a specific protocol that at scale keeps costs low. Most of you fail to practice detachment. You stay so attached to a specific creative. You refuse to let it go. Okay? Remake it. Change your shirt. Wear a fucking hat. Change the color of the text in the image. Increase the caption size. Add B-roll. Change the B-roll you added. Shoot on multiple camera angles and give yourself more opportunities for how you can cut the video. Okay?
[140:20] Do not stay attached to a creative for too long of a period of time. That generally inflates your cost dramatically. Why? Have you ever opened your phone? Don't fish for that iPad. Okay, we're chilling now. Have you ever opened your phone, opened a social app, and seen the same piece of content as you just did a couple moments ago when you previously opened it?
[140:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Platform Content Variation Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Representação de feed social variado, algoritmo de plataforma, penalização por repetição
[140:55] As you did the couple moments before that when you opened it. Yes or no? Yes. And you swipe and you fucking leave if you do, right? Why do they do that? It's not. They want to keep you on the platform as much as possible. Okay? So, they always vary the content you see. They try to serve you more things based on what you've consumed and spent the most time looking at. Why? Because they want you to keep scrolling. So, they make more money when they serve more ads. Okay?
[141:30] Now, think about you and your attachments to a specific creative, how that plays into that. Why do they punish you by increasing your cost per result when you just won't fucking let go of that single piece of creative? Because you're not following the fucking rule of the platform, dude. Okay? You got to follow the rules of the platform.
[142:00] This protocol of overcoming ad fatigue by no way, shape, or form says, "Hey, just keep running the same fucking creative forever." No, it's the exact opposite. It says overcome fatigue by detaching from creative and making new stuff. Okay? I openly tell you, you can remake the stuff that works, but do not run the same exact fucking I mean, with literal no changes for long durations of time and then message me and ask, "Oh, dude, my costs are going up. Why are they going up?" Okay, I'm going to send you this every time. Does that make sense?
[142:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Holiday & Macro Event Impact"
NÚMEROS: April Fool's Day, Easter, Valentine's Day, Super Bowl, President's Day, St. Patrick's Day, Spring Break
VISUAL: Calendário de holidays impactando custos, gráfico de inflação por evento, contexto macro-econômico
[142:45] Very good. This is in addition to that one of the other things that we're seeing that's really impacting performance right now and it is the holidays. So there have been some super random holidays. I'm talking like April Fool's Day caused some inflation. Easter right now is causing some inflation. Do you guys remember how much inflation there was for Valentine's Day, uh, Super Bowl, President's Day, and then right after that period of time, costs dropped again?
[143:20] We had inflation again right around St. Patrick's Day. We had inflation around spring break. Okay, these things that historically didn't matter at all matter so much now. This is the most recent few days charted with a cost per call to the NASDAQ. There were news articles that I pulled for this specific client as well that directly correlated to this extreme fluctuation.
[143:50] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Tariff Impact Case Study"
NÚMEROS: $357 baseline CPL, $1,100 CPL (tariff announcement day), $278 CPL (rebound), $484 CPL (125% China tariffs), $149 CPL (bounce), $270 CPL recent
VISUAL: Gráfico detalhado de CPL correlacionado com NASDAQ e anúncios de tarifa, extrema volatilidade documentada
[143:55] Let me read it for you because it's kind of hard to see. This is a $357 cost per call, which is high for this client. Okay? It didn't make sense to be up there, but during this period of time when it jumped to 362, it jumped up there because there was a bunch of news about impending tariffs. Nobody knew what was going to happen. Then the tariff announcement came. Anybody remember that little visual of Trump holding the giant cards with the tariffs, the retalatory tariffs? You guys remember that?
[144:30] That was this day when the market absolutely bottomed out. I had a $1,100 cost per call that day. It was 360 the day before. What caused that drop? Tariffs. Is it safe to say tariffs impacted my cost per call? The market then rebounds. I'm at 278 per call the very next day. Not a change in ads. Nothing other than the macro environment I was operating in influencing my cost per result.
[145:05] The market then fucking bottoms out again due to more fear. This is the day that Trump said he was doing 125% tariffs on China. China 484 cost per call. Absolutely fucking mega bounce. $149 cost per call. That was sweet, by the way. Made a lot of money there. $270 was the most recent cost per call when I made this uh was it last night or the night before? I think it was the night before. I made this on Thursday.
[145:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Context-Based Creative Solution"
NÚMEROS: Recent drop to $80 CPL after contextual ad
VISUAL: Solução de contexto vs ad fatigue, gráfico de recovery pós-creative contextual, estratégia de addressing macro concerns
[145:45] I sent it to a client and I said, "Tariffs are impacting your cost per call. I need you to film some ads that create some context that now is still a good time to do what you talk about doing in this kind of environment because people are clearly very scared relative to the market conditions. It's making their decision to opt in or not. It's making their decision to buy.
[146:20] Have you mapped your cost per call to the randomness of tariffs yet? Have you seen and been self-aware of the fluctuations in cost relative to random non historically relevant holidays? I have never in my fucking 12 years advertising seen fucking Valentine's Day or St. Patrick's Day fuck my cost per result. It's been insane this year how much these non-significant things have impacted us.
[146:55] So again, my point is context, not necessarily ad fatigue. Context is what solves that problem. That particular client on Thursday night when they received that message, they filmed an ad the next day. The ad was executed extremely well. It talked about tariffs and it talked about how now is still a good time to do it. Over the last 48 hours, there's still been turbulent news. My costs had gone down to 80 again. I haven't seen 80 since this guy got in office, man.
[147:30] Okay, context, not necessarily ad fatigue, but context can solve the problem. Does that make sense? Okay, very good. Let's go and uh let's go get some lunch.
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
Josh: $450K → $800K/mês (quase dobrou) fazendo 2x webinars por semana
Devin: $75K → $400K/mês através de sales calls e mais ad spend
Challenge case study: $300K ad spend, 1.9x ROAS front-end, $3.4M back-end revenue
Daniel challenge: $2K spend = $200K profit (planejando apenas $15K no próximo)
Cliente warm webinar: crescimento Instagram 2% com cold ads vs 0.6% apenas warm
YouTube: 2 horas/semana = dezenas de milhares em revenue por hora
Email open rates: 60-70% como novo benchmark
Cliente $1.4M/mês: 40% target margin vs 20-30% durante scaling
Tariff impact: $357 CPL → $1,100 CPL → $149 CPL (extrema volatilidade)
YouTube production: $3K/mês para equipe completa (editing, thumbnails, posting)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Webinars:
1x mensal WARM ($30-50K spend, centenas de milhares de audiência)
1x semanal COLD (mínimo) ou 2x semanal (optimal)
Total: 5 webinars/mês (4 cold + 1 warm) = baseline
Múltiplas ofertas = dobrar webinars (10/mês com 2 ofertas)
LIVE webinars > evergreen atualmente
Nunca promover frequência real (usar scarcity)
Challenge Funnels:
Quarterly execution (não frequente)
3-day format (3-4 horas/dia) > 5-day para busy rich people
Front-end: ticket sales (pode ser break-even ou lucrativo)
Back-end: onde o dinheiro real acontece
Estrutura: Day 1 (content), Day 2 (pre-frame + pitch + Q&A), Day 3 (rep pitch + Q&A)
Guest speakers opcionais (sem pitch direto)
House money strategy: profit do primeiro = investimento no segundo
Group Sales Calls:
2-3x por semana em horários diferentes
Owner ou superstar closer (não reps normais)
2 dozen people máximo
Private DM option no Zoom
Business phone line ($30/mês) para follow-up
Q&A format, não hard pitch
Immediate closer follow-up após call
Call Funnels:
30-day vs 60-day look-back windows (ROI cresce com tempo)
10-day buying frame (in or out)
Cost of growth vs true ROAS (subtrair scaled dollars)
Judge por credit card billing cycle
Follow-up crítico para conversões tardias
YouTube Strategy:
2 horas domingo para filmar 2 vídeos
10-20% conhecimento free, 80-90% paid
"Edging" strategy (não dar tudo)
Pitch: primeiro terço, segundo terço, final
Chapter markers para navegação
Não focar em views (1K-4K é suficiente)
$3K/mês offshore team
Ideation: reflexão sobre client results
Confirmation Pages:
Video no topo (due diligence framing)
Testimonials + breakout videos (2-5 min cada)
Most common questions primeiro
2 videos por row (desktop), stacked (mobile)
Thumbnail = question being answered
Speed to lead concept aplicado ao marketing
Email Strategy:
60-70% open rates = novo benchmark
Value-dense: link para consumables (videos, docs, long-form)
Alternative conversion mechanisms (cross-promote funnels)
Frequency testing: aumentar até open rates caírem
4-6 emails/dia possível se value-dense
Ad Fatigue Protocol:
Detachment de criativos específicos
Remake com variações (shirt, hat, text color, B-roll, angles)
Nunca rodar mesmo criativo por muito tempo
Plataformas penalizam repetição
Context > fatigue (address macro events)
Reddit Strategy:
Impacta principalmente show rates
Proativo: criar thread, pedir reviews honestas
Downvotes + bot accusations = killer breakout video
Documentar: honest request + real responses + false accusations
Escolher subreddit correto (não nests de haters)
Testimonials:
Nome visível + social profile clicável
Contactable strategy (não pagar)
Respeitar boundaries por tipo de cliente
FDC compliance: disclose se pagar
ESTRUTURA:
Webinar Replenishment Cycle:
Cold feeds warm (como ciclo da água)
4 cold + 1 warm = sustainable growth
Warm only = drought (fonte seca)
Monthly warm = prevents fatigue
Weekly cold = consistency
Highest Dollar Value Actions Framework:
Identificar top 3 ações diárias
Não virtue signal ou adicionar biases
Exemplos: webinars, sales calls, YouTube, training team
Mr. Mini Elon Musk problem: $600K = disengagement threshold
Devin example: sales calls = primary driver
10-Day Buying Frame:
Commitment window estabelecido upfront
In or out by day 10
Real reason = única exceção
Miss deadline = permanent DQ (no second chances)
Belief system, não sales tactic
House Money Concept:
Original investment volta ao bolso
Profit = house money (different risk tolerance)
Gamble mais agressivamente com profit
Daniel example: $2K → $200K, deveria investir $198K no próximo
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Challenge ROI Breakdown:
Case 1: $300K spend, 1.9x front-end, $3.4M back-end ($10K offer, $2K deposit)
Case 2 (Daniel): $2K spend, $200K profit (100x ROI)
Próximo planejado: $15K (deveria ser $70K+ ou $198K)
Webinar Economics:
Josh: 2x/semana = $450K → $800K
Warm spend: $30-50K (centenas de milhares audience)
Cold spend: proporcional à audience size
YouTube ROI:
2 horas/semana = 8 horas/mês
Dezenas de milhares revenue/hora
$3K/mês production cost
Dinner money comparison: $2K La Tellier
Profit Margins Durante Growth:
Target: 40% profit margin
Reality durante scaling: 20-30%
Trade-off: growth vs optimization
Cost of growth embedded em ROAS
Ad Cost Volatility (Tariff Impact):
Baseline: $357 CPL
Tariff announcement: $1,100 CPL (3x spike)
Rebound: $278 CPL
China 125% tariffs: $484 CPL
Post-context ad: $149 CPL
Recent: $270 CPL
Best recent: $80 CPL (não visto desde nova administração)
Email Performance:
60-70% open rates (novo norm)
Frequency: 4-6 emails/dia testável
Value-dense + consumables = key
Client Growth Examples:
Josh: $450K → $800K (77% growth)
Devin: $75K → $400K (433% growth)
Missed opportunity client: $1.5M/mês potential (cortou oferta 3:1 ROAS)
DURAÇÃO: ~147 minutos (2h27min) FORMATO: Mastermind presencial com apresentação, exemplos ao vivo, Q&A interativa, exercícios práticos, distribuição de prêmios (iPhones, iPads)
